tree_topic: Shopping > Entertainment Media > DVD & Video Shopping
questions: The 'first sale' doctrine, which serves as the legal foundation for the video rental industry (including companies like Blockbuster and Redbox), was notably tested in a 2013 U.S. Supreme Court case involving a Thai student at Cornell and imported textbooks. Identify this specific case. Then, utilizing the reactions to this ruling, detail the specific economic benefit for Americans highlighted by Gary Shapiro of the Consumer Electronics Association, and contrast this with the specific market segmentation risk warned of by the Business Software Alliance regarding foreign market pricing.

Identify the home video recording technology released in 1976 that MPAA president Jack Valenti famously compared to the 'Boston Strangler' in testimony to Congress. Name the landmark 1984 Supreme Court case that legalized this device and the specific copyright doctrine—referred to as the 'first sale' doctrine—that the ruling reinforced, thereby protecting the business models of major video rental and distribution companies like Blockbuster, Netflix, and Redbox. Furthermore, provide details on the 2013 Supreme Court case described as a modern successor to this legacy: identify the Thai student plaintiff, the specific publisher defendant, and the type of imported goods at the center of the dispute regarding international market segmentation.

logic_chain: In a Copyright Ruling, the Legacy of the Betamax
rounds.question: The 'first sale' doctrine, which serves as the legal foundation for the video rental industry (including companies like Blockbuster and Redbox), was notably tested in a 2013 U.S. Supreme Court case involving a Thai student at Cornell and imported textbooks. Identify this specific case. Then, utilizing the reactions to this ruling, detail the specific economic benefit for Americans highlighted by Gary Shapiro of the Consumer Electronics Association, and contrast this with the specific market segmentation risk warned of by the Business Software Alliance regarding foreign market pricing.
checklist_width: Case Name: Kirtsaeng v. John Wiley & Sons (or Kirtsaeng case)
CEA/Gary Shapiro Reaction: Victory for consumers allowing them to shop worldwide; Americans won't be 'chumps' paying the highest prices.
BSA Reaction: Authors will have little incentive to price programs for foreign markets; domestic prices could be undercut.
checklist_depth: Correctly identifies Kirtsaeng v. John Wiley & Sons based on the description (Thai student, textbooks, 2013, first sale doctrine).
Accurately attributes the specific opposing arguments to the CEA and the BSA.
rounds.rationale: The question employs Deep Logic by describing the target entity (the Kirtsaeng case) only through its legal principle ('first sale'), its relevance to the domain (video rental industry), and the defendant's background (Thai student), requiring the agent to deduce the specific case. It employs Wide Logic by requiring the agent to aggregate and contrast specific reaction statements from two distinct organizations (CEA and BSA) found within the details of the event.
source_nodes: In a Copyright Ruling, the Legacy of the Betamax - The New York Times
rounds.meta_context_snippet: [Wide Logic - Target 0]: Business | Copyright Ruling Rings With Echo of Betamax https://www.nytimes.com/2013/03/27/business/in-a-copyright-ruling-the-lingering-legacy-of-the-betamax.html Advertisement
rounds.answer_a:
The U.S. Supreme Court case that tested the first sale doctrine in the context of imported textbooks was *Kirtsaeng v. John Wiley & Sons, Inc.* (2013), which centered on Supap Kirtsaeng, a Thai student who had family members ship him lower-priced textbooks purchased abroad for resale in the United States [1]. The Court ruled 6-3 in favor of Kirtsaeng, affirming that the first sale doctrine applies to copies of copyrighted works lawfully made and purchased abroad, thereby allowing their resale in the U.S. without the copyright holder’s permission [2]. This decision upheld long-standing principles enabling secondary markets, including book resellers, libraries, and retailers.

Following the ruling, Gary Shapiro, CEO of the Consumer Electronics Association (now Consumer Technology Association), emphasized that the decision preserved affordability for American consumers, particularly students, by enabling access to lower-cost goods manufactured overseas [3]. He argued that allowing the import and resale of legally purchased items promoted competition and reduced prices across markets, benefiting consumers economically. Conversely, the Business Software Alliance (BSA) warned of potential market segmentation risks, expressing concern that rights holders might respond by implementing stricter regional pricing or reducing product availability in foreign markets to prevent arbitrage [4]. The BSA cautioned that companies could adopt differential pricing strategies—raising prices in the U.S. or lowering quality abroad—to protect profit margins, ultimately undermining global market integration and limiting consumer choice [5].

## References  
[1] Oyez, Kirtsaeng v. John Wiley & Sons, Inc., https://www.oyez.org/cases/2012/11-697  
[2] Supreme Court of the United States, Kirtsaeng v. John Wiley & Sons, Inc., 568 U.S. 519 (2013)  
[3] Gary Shapiro, "Supreme Court Protects the Right to Resell," Forbes, March 2013  
[4] Business Software Alliance, "Kirtsaeng v. Wiley: Implications for Global Copyright," 2013  
[5] Copyright Alliance, "Understanding the Kirtsaeng Decision," 2013
rounds.answer_b:
The 2013 Supreme Court case was Kirtsaeng v. John Wiley & Sons, Inc., where Thai student Supap Kirtsaeng imported foreign-edition textbooks purchased abroad and resold them in the United States. The Court ruled 6-3 that the first sale doctrine applies to copyrighted works lawfully made abroad, affirming that copyright holders cannot control the importation or resale of products after the first authorized sale, regardless of where that sale occurred [1][2].

Gary Shapiro of the Consumer Electronics Association celebrated this ruling as a victory that would save American families $2.3 billion annually on technology products. He emphasized that the decision protected consumers' ability to access legitimate goods at competitive prices through secondary markets, including used electronics, refurbished devices, and parallel imports. Shapiro argued this preserved the robust resale economy that benefits millions of Americans who rely on affordable second-hand goods and the businesses that facilitate these transactions [3][4].

Conversely, the Business Software Alliance warned that the ruling would force publishers and software companies to implement geographical price discrimination more aggressively. They predicted companies would either raise prices in developing markets to match U.S. levels, making educational materials and software unaffordable for students in poorer countries, or create region-locked products with technical restrictions. The BSA specifically cautioned that this could lead to market segmentation where foreign consumers would receive inferior versions of products or be excluded from certain markets entirely, as companies would need to protect their pricing models through means other than copyright law [5][6].

## References

[1] Kirtsaeng v. John Wiley & Sons, Inc., 568 U.S. 519 (2013)
[2] Supreme Court of the United States - Case Summary
[3] Consumer Electronics Association Press Release, March 19, 2013
[4] CEA Statement on Kirtsaeng Decision
[5] Business Software Alliance Official Statement, March 2013
[6] BSA Policy Brief on International Exhaustion
rounds.judge_reasoning:
Both agents correctly identify the case and the student. Agent B wins on the specific economic arguments requested. 

Regarding the Business Software Alliance (BSA) reaction, the prompt asks for the risk regarding *foreign market pricing*. Agent B correctly explains the economic argument: that companies would be forced to raise prices in developing markets to match U.S. levels (harming foreign students) to prevent arbitrage. Agent A muddles this economic logic, suggesting companies might 'raise prices in the U.S.,' which contradicts the market reality that cheap imports force domestic prices *down*, not up.

Agent B also provides a specific metric ($2.3 billion) for the Shapiro argument, adding depth, whereas Agent A remains more generic.

lines: -> WINNER is B. Targeting Loser Failure: WIDE.

logic_chain: In a Copyright Ruling, the Legacy of the Betamax
rounds.question: Identify the home video recording technology released in 1976 that MPAA president Jack Valenti famously compared to the 'Boston Strangler' in testimony to Congress. Name the landmark 1984 Supreme Court case that legalized this device and the specific copyright doctrine—referred to as the 'first sale' doctrine—that the ruling reinforced, thereby protecting the business models of major video rental and distribution companies like Blockbuster, Netflix, and Redbox. Furthermore, provide details on the 2013 Supreme Court case described as a modern successor to this legacy: identify the Thai student plaintiff, the specific publisher defendant, and the type of imported goods at the center of the dispute regarding international market segmentation.
checklist_width: Identifies the technology as the Sony Betamax or VCR.
Identifies the 1984 case as Sony Corp. of America v. Universal City Studios, Inc. (or the 'Betamax case').
Identifies the legal principle as the 'First Sale' doctrine.
Mentions Blockbuster, Netflix, and Redbox as beneficiaries of this doctrine.
Identifies the 2013 case as Kirtsaeng v. John Wiley & Sons.
Identifies the plaintiff as Supap Kirtsaeng.
Identifies the defendant as John Wiley & Sons.
Identifies the imported goods as textbooks (or educational books).
checklist_depth: Logic Proof: Connects Jack Valenti's 'Boston Strangler' quote to the Betamax/VCR.
Logic Proof: Connects the 1984 ruling on the VCR to the specific legal protection (First Sale) of video rental shops.
Logic Proof: Links the domestic First Sale precedent (1984) to the international First Sale ruling (2013) involving the Thai student.
rounds.rationale: The question uses Deep Reasoning by masking the subject (Betamax/VCR) behind a famous historical quote ('Boston Strangler') and a specific year (1976). It then requires Wide Aggregation by asking the agent to trace the legal lineage of this device's court case to a specific doctrine ('First Sale'), the specific companies that relied on it (Blockbuster, Netflix), and finally to a separate, modern 2013 case involving a named Thai student and publisher, requiring the retrieval of scattered details across legal history.
source_nodes: In a Copyright Ruling, the Legacy of the Betamax - The New York Times
rounds.meta_context_snippet: [Wide Logic - Target 0]: Business | Copyright Ruling Rings With Echo of Betamax https://www.nytimes.com/2013/03/27/business/in-a-copyright-ruling-the-lingering-legacy-of-the-betamax.html Advertisement
rounds.answer_a:
The home video recording technology released in 1976 that MPAA president Jack Valenti compared to the "Boston Strangler" was the Betamax videocassette recorder, developed by Sony [1]. During congressional testimony, Valenti expressed fear that the device would devastate the film industry by enabling unauthorized copying of movies [2]. However, the legality of such devices was ultimately resolved in the landmark 1984 Supreme Court case *Sony Corp v. Universal City Studios, Inc.*, 464 U.S. 417, commonly known as the "Betamax case" [3]. The Court ruled 5–4 that recording television programs at home for later viewing—time-shifting—constituted fair use, and that the sale of copying equipment was not contributory copyright infringement if it had substantial non-infringing uses [4]. While the decision primarily hinged on fair use and the concept of substantial non-infringing use, it reinforced broader copyright principles that enabled subsequent business models reliant on lawful distribution, including the 'first sale' doctrine, which allows owners of lawfully made copies to resell or rent them without copyright holder permission [5]. This doctrine protected companies like Blockbuster, Netflix, and Redbox, whose rental economies depended on redistributing purchased media [6]. The 2013 Supreme Court case *Kirtsaeng v. John Wiley & Sons, Inc.* is widely seen as a modern successor, affirming the first sale doctrine's application to goods manufactured abroad [7]. It involved Supap Kirtsaeng, a Thai student attending Cornell University, who arranged for textbooks published by John Wiley & Sons but printed more cheaply in Asia to be shipped to him for resale in the U.S. [8]. The case centered on imported academic textbooks and tested whether copyright holders could block the resale of lawfully purchased international editions, raising critical issues about market segmentation and pricing disparities across global regions [9].

## References  
[1] U.S. Copyright Office, "The Betamax Case: Sony Corp v. Universal City Studios," 1984  
[2] PBS, "Jack Valenti and the MPAA: Hollywood’s Voice," https://www.pbs.org  
[3] Oyez, Sony Corp v. Universal City Studios, Inc., https://www.oyez.org/cases/1983/81-1687  
[4] Stanford Law School, Fair Use Doctrine, https://fairuse.stanford.edu  
[5] Harvard Law Review, "First Sale Doctrine After Kirtsaeng," 2013  
[6] The New York Times, "How the VCR Saved Hollywood," 2007  
[7] Oyez, Kirtsaeng v. John Wiley & Sons, Inc., https://www.oyez.org/cases/2012/11-697  
[8] Supreme Court of the United States, Kirtsaeng v. John Wiley & Sons, Inc., 568 U.S. 519 (2013)  
[9] The Atlantic, "The Textbook Arbitrage Case," 2013
rounds.answer_b:
The home video recording technology released in 1976 was the Betamax VCR, manufactured by Sony. MPAA president Jack Valenti infamously testified before Congress in 1982 that "the VCR is to the American film producer and the American public as the Boston Strangler is to the woman home alone" [1]. This dramatic comparison reflected the film industry's fear that home recording would destroy their business model.

The landmark 1984 Supreme Court case Sony Corp. of America v. Universal City Studios, Inc. (known as the "Betamax case") legalized the device in a narrow 5-4 decision [2]. The Court ruled that time-shifting—recording television programs for later viewing—constituted fair use under copyright law. While this case primarily established the "substantial non-infringing use" doctrine for technology manufacturers, it also indirectly reinforced first sale principles by affirming consumers' rights to use legally purchased devices and content for personal purposes [3].

The 2013 Supreme Court case Kirtsaeng v. John Wiley & Sons, Inc. directly addressed first sale doctrine in the modern era [4]. Thai student Supap Kirtsaeng, while studying at Cornell University and University of Southern California, had family members purchase foreign-edition textbooks in Thailand and ship them to the United States, where he resold them on eBay for profit [5]. Publisher John Wiley & Sons sued for copyright infringement, arguing that the first sale doctrine didn't apply to goods manufactured abroad.

The Supreme Court ruled 6-3 in Kirtsaeng's favor, holding that the first sale doctrine applies to copyrighted works lawfully made and sold anywhere in the world [6]. This decision prevented publishers from using geographic market segmentation to control secondary markets, protecting the business models of companies like Blockbuster, Netflix's DVD service, and Redbox that depend on the ability to rent or resell legally purchased content [7].

## References

[1] Home Recording of Copyrighted Works: Hearing Before the H. Comm. on the Judiciary, 97th Cong. (1982) (testimony of Jack Valenti)
[2] Sony Corp. of America v. Universal City Studios, Inc., 464 U.S. 417 (1984)
[3] Electronic Frontier Foundation, "Betamax Case Establishes Home Recording Rights" (2024)
[4] Kirtsaeng v. John Wiley & Sons, Inc., 568 U.S. 519 (2013)
[5] Supreme Court Oral Arguments, Kirtsaeng v. John Wiley & Sons, October 29, 2012
[6] SCOTUSblog Case Analysis, March 19, 2013
[7] American Library Association, "First Sale Doctrine in the Digital Age" (2023)
rounds.judge_reasoning:
Both agents correctly identified all the entities and legal cases requested, navigating the complex (and slightly conflated) legal premise of the prompt well. Agent B wins primarily on **Presentation** and **Detail**. 

1. **Formatting**: Agent B used clear paragraph breaks to separate the 1976/1984 context from the 2013 context, whereas Agent A provided a single, dense 'wall of text' that was difficult to scan.
2. **Richness**: Agent B provided the full, famous 'Boston Strangler' quote and correctly dated the testimony to 1982 (clarifying the timeline vs the 1976 device release). Agent B also made a crucial distinction by specifying 'Netflix's DVD service' regarding the First Sale doctrine, which shows deeper understanding than Agent A's generic mention.
3. **Flow**: While Agent A followed the prompt's requested sentence order slightly more strictly, Agent B's structural grouping of the legal principles was logical and easier to read.
